title: Paper Explained: Rhetorical Questions in LLM Representations: A Linear Probing Study - A Beginner's Guide
subtitle: How LLMs Signal Rhetorical Questions

content.background:
Before this work, we didn’t really know how large language models internally handle rhetorical questions. These are the kinds of questions asked to persuade or signal a stance, not to get an answer. People often assume there might be one clear “signal” inside the model that marks something as rhetorical and that this signal would be the same across different kinds of text. But there wasn’t solid evidence about whether such a simple, universal cue exists, where in the model it appears during processing, or how easy it is to read out with simple tests.

Another big open question was about context and transfer. If you train a small diagnostic test on one social-media dataset, will that same test work on another dataset with different ways of arguing? It’s not obvious, because different discourse contexts might use different cues: some signals come from the overall flow of a debate, while others come from eye-catching punctuation or sentence structure. So researchers wondered whether there’s a single shared “rhetorical direction” in the model, or a mix of directions that depend on the particular text the model is looking at.

Why this matters is practical as well as scholarly. Rhetorical questions are a common tool in persuasive writing and online debate, so understanding how a large language model represents them helps us anticipate how these models might influence or interpret human discourse. It also informs how we design tools for moderation, safety, or analysis of argumentative text. If the model uses multiple cues that vary by context, then simple, one-size-fits-all detectors may miss important nuances—and recognizing that complexity is the first step toward more reliable, interpretable AI systems.
content.methodology:
This paper asks: how do large language models internally signal a rhetorical question, i.e., a question used to persuade or stance-signaling rather than to seek information? The authors test this by using a simple, interpretable tool called a linear probe. Think of a linear probe as a tiny classifier that tries to read a label directly from a fixed representation produced by the model. If the probe can separate “rhetorical question” from “information-seeking question” with a straight line in that representation space, it suggests the model has encoded that distinction in an accessible way. They study this across two social-media datasets that sit in different discourse contexts, and pay special attention to which part of the model’s output (especially the last token) carries the signal.

What they did, step by step:
- They gather two datasets of questions from social media, each with different ways rhetoric shows up (discourse context matters).
- They freeze the model and extract representations from its layers, focusing on the representations associated with the questions, particularly the final token.
- They train simple linear probes to distinguish rhetorical questions from information-seeking questions using those representations, testing both within each dataset and in cross-dataset transfer.
- They compare where in the model the signal shows up (early layers vs. late layers, last token vs. others) and how robust the signal is when you move the probe from one dataset to the other.

Key findings in plain terms:
- Signals appear early in the processing and are most stably captured by the representations for the last token of the input. In other words, the model’s final word in a question tends to hold the clearest clue about whether the speaker is being rhetorical.
- Within each dataset, rhetorical vs. information-seeking questions are linearly separable, meaning a simple linear probe can reliably tell them apart.
- When you train a probe on one dataset and test it on the other (cross-dataset transfer), you still get useful detection (AUROC around 0.7–0.8), but the signal isn’t the same everywhere. Probes trained on different datasets rank examples differently, and the overlap among top-ranked instances is surprisingly low (often below 0.2).

A useful takeaway is that rhetorical questions in LLMs aren’t encoded along a single universal direction. The qualitative analysis shows at least two distinct rhetorical phenomena: some probes pick up discourse-level stance that threads through extended argumentation, while others pick up more localized, syntax-driven questioning acts. Put simply, the model encodes rhetorical intent in multiple linear directions, each emphasizing different cues. This means interpretability via probing reveals a multi-faceted picture: there isn’t one boss cue for “rhetorical questioning,” but a family of cues distributed across different parts of the model’s representations.
content.results:
This study asks: how do large language models (LLMs) internally recognize rhetorical questions—questions asked to persuade or signal a stance, not to seek information? The researchers used a simple, transparent method called linear probing: they trained straight-line classifiers to separate rhetorical questions from information-seeking ones based on the model’s internal representations. They tested this on two social-media datasets that have different kinds of discourse, to see if the signals are consistent across contexts. They found that hints of rhetoric show up early in the model’s processing and that the last token’s representation is the most reliable place to read those hints. In short, the model already contains rhetorical clues early on, and the final token carries a stable signal.

Within each dataset, rhetorical questions are linearly separable from information-seeking questions, meaning a simple linear boundary can distinguish them in the model’s representation. When you try to transfer a probe trained on one dataset to the other, you still get some detectable signal, but it’s not perfect. Importantly, probes trained on different datasets don’t agree on exactly which specific examples are the strongest indicators of rhetoric—the overlap among top cues is often quite small. Qualitative analysis shows this happens because rhetorical language is diverse: some probes pick up broader, discourse-level signals embedded in longer arguments, while others focus on more local, syntax-driven question forms.

The big takeaway is that rhetorical questions in LLM representations are not encoded along a single, shared direction. Instead, multiple linear directions capture different aspects of rhetoric—some related to the writer’s stance in an argument, others to the grammar of asking a question. This matters practically because it informs how we build tools to detect or analyze rhetorical language in AI systems: relying on one simple cue is likely to miss the full picture, and using a combination of cues (and paying attention to last-token signals) can yield more robust results. The work also clarifies why cross-dataset transfers are imperfect: useful signals exist, but they’re context-specific, reflecting rich, varied ways rhetoric appears in real text.
content.significance:
This paper matters today because it tackles a very practical question: how do large language models actually represent rhetorical devices like rhetorical questions inside their hidden layers? The authors show that these cues aren’t stored as one simple signal you can grab with a single vector. Instead, there are multiple linear directions that capture different aspects of rhetoric, and the signals show up very early in the model’s processing, with the last token often carrying the most stable cue. For students, this means interpretability of LLMs is more nuanced than “one hidden direction equals one concept.” It also explains why simple probing tests can be imperfect: you may need several probes to detect different facets of discourse, and even then the results can diverge depending on the data you test.

In the long run, this work helps shape how we build, audit, and govern modern AI systems. It suggests that models encode complex social and discourse cues in distributed, multi-directional ways, which has direct implications for safety, alignment, and fairness. Applications include tools for content moderation and misinformation detection, discourse and stance analysis in social media, and educational or legal tech that analyzes argument quality and rhetorical strategy. It also pushes the field toward more robust interpretability practices: instead of looking for a single “rhetoric vector,” researchers and engineers should design multi-probe, cross-domain evaluations to understand how models handle persuasion, argumentation, and discourse across contexts. The finding that cross-dataset transfer works at a measurable level but with different probe rankings reminds us to be cautious about assuming a shared representation; this nuance matters when building generalizable safety and auditing tools for AI.

Connecting to modern AI systems people use every day—ChatGPT, Claude, Bard, and other conversational agents—the paper’s lessons are highly relevant. These systems routinely generate or respond to rhetorical turns in dialogue, and understanding that rhetorical signals are distributed across multiple directions helps explain why detecting or controlling such maneuvers is tricky in practice. The work motivates discourse-aware safety and explainability tools: systems that can audit their own responses for rhetorical manipulation, or that can explain to users which rhetorical cues influenced an answer. In short, the paper lays groundwork for more robust, responsible, and transparent dialogue AI by showing that rhetorical phenomena in LLMs are rich, multi-faceted, and not reducible to a single direction or feature.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding Linear Probing: The Heart of Rhetorical Questions in LLM Representations
concept_explanation.content:
Analogy to start: Think of a big library (the LLM) and a tiny, simple filter (the linear probe). You want to know if the library stores a special kind of sentence called a rhetorical question. Instead of reading every page, you run each sentence through the filter, which only looks at a fixed, simple summary of the sentence (a representation). If the filter can reliably tell rhetorical questions from ordinary questions just from that summary, you’ve shown that the library already contains a detectable signal in a way you can read with a tiny, straightforward test.

Here’s how linear probing works in this paper, step by step. First, you pick a way to summarize each sentence from the LLM, such as the last token’s hidden state (the representation that the model has at the end of the sentence). Then you freeze the LLM—leave its weights fixed so you’re testing what it already knows, not changing its mind. Next, you assemble a labeled set of sentences: some are rhetorical questions (meant to persuade or signal stance) and some are information-seeking questions (asked to obtain facts). For each sentence, you feed it through the LLM to get its representation. You train a simple linear classifier (like logistic regression) that takes that representation as input and predicts whether the sentence is rhetorical or not. Finally you test the classifier on new sentences to see how well it can separate the two kinds. The study compares different representations (for example, last-token vs other parts of the sentence) and checks how well the probe works across different datasets.

Concrete examples help. Suppose you have these two sentences: “Isn’t this amazing?” (rhetorical) and “What is the capital of France?” (information-seeking). After you pass them through the model, the last-token representation for each sentence yields a numeric vector. The linear probe learns a weight vector that, when dotted with that vector (plus a bias), gives a high score for rhetorical questions and a low score for information-seeking ones. If the probe is effective, you’ll get an AUROC well above chance (in this study, around 0.7–0.8 when tested across datasets), meaning the probe can distinguish the two kinds fairly well even though it’s just a simple linear rule. Interestingly, the study finds that the last-token representation often carries the strongest, most stable signal for this task.

Why this matters is twofold. First, linear probes give a clear, easy-to-interpret test: is there a line in the model’s internal space that separates rhetorical from information-seeking questions? If yes, you know the model encodes that information in a way that a simple line can read. Second, the results show that there isn’t just one secret direction in the model for rhetoric. Different probes trained on different datasets can highlight different cues—some capture larger, discourse-level stance, others pick up more on local syntax. So even when a probe trained on one dataset transfers reasonably well to another, the exact “best direction” can differ, and the ranking of which sentences are most rhetorical can change a lot depending on which data you trained the probe with. This teaches a broader lesson: neural representations can encode a phenomenon like rhetoric along multiple directions, reflecting different aspects of how people use language.

In terms of practical use, linear probes are a handy tool for AI researchers and educators. They let you peek inside a model to see whether it has learned a particular kind of knowledge (like rhetorical signaling) without changing the model itself. This can help in auditing models for safety or bias, guiding prompt design (understanding what cues the model responds to), or diagnosing why an LLM might misinterpret a rhetorical sentence as a factual one. The key takeaway is that rhetorical questions in LLM representations can be detected with simple, linear boundaries, but those boundaries are not unique or universal—there are multiple, meaningful directions in the model’s space, and which one you pick matters for your task and dataset.

summary: This paper shows that rhetorical questions in large language models are encoded along multiple distinct linear directions, detectable early by linear probes (especially in last-token representations) across two social-media datasets, indicating no single shared signal but multiple context-dependent cues.
